steps.title: Identify the two clauses surrounding the blank
steps.content: Before the blank: “The Limon technique... is known for its emphasis on breath control and its interplay of weight and [blank]” — this is an independent clause (IC) with its own subject and verb. After the blank: “dancers may explore, for example, the moment of mid-air suspension at the top of a jump” — this is also an independent clause. Two ICs joined at the blank require a semicolon, a period, or a comma plus a coordinating conjunction. A comma alone creates a comma splice; no punctuation creates a fused sentence.

steps.title: Evaluate each choice against the joining rule
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'weightlessness;': a semicolon correctly joins two independent clauses — grammatically valid. ✓
  • Choice B — 'weightlessness which': “which” without a preceding comma attempts to introduce a relative clause, but it runs the second IC directly into the first without proper punctuation, creating a run-on. ✗
  • Choice C — 'weightlessness,': a comma alone between two ICs is a comma splice. ✗
  • Choice D — 'weightlessness': no punctuation at all between two ICs creates a fused sentence. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
